title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Senators target data center power and water use as towns weigh pauses amid noise fights.

lines.text: Good evening, and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're digging into new bills that would regulate how much power and water these facilities can pull, right as several communities push back with recalls and noise complaints.
That sounds like the biggest development we've seen in a while. What's the core of these bills, Graham?
Democratic senators introduced measures aimed at energy costs and water consumption for data centers. They're responding to the rapid growth and the strain it's putting on local systems.
Why now? Have we seen this kind of push before?
This builds on earlier local fights. We've had townships debating zoning and moratoriums for months, but state-level rules on power and water are newer.
What exactly would these bills change for a typical project?
They'd set limits or reporting requirements on how much electricity and water a facility can use, which could affect everything from site selection to how much locals pay on their utility bills.
That ties right into the electric bills issue. How much more could residents see if these centers keep expanding unchecked?
The concern is that heavy power demand could drive up rates for everyone on the grid. These facilities are huge consumers, and without rules, the costs get spread around.
And water? We've heard stories about ponds dropping during construction.
Exactly. One recent case showed a neighborhood pond recovering after excavation work, but it highlighted how quickly aquifers can be affected. The bills would try to get ahead of that.
Who are the main players here? The senators, or are local officials and companies weighing in too?

It's a mix. The Democratic sponsors are leading at the state level, while towns like those in Kent County are dealing with recall petitions over a Microsoft proposal and noise issues in places like Dowagiac.
The Dowagiac shift from Bitcoin mining after complaints — does that show these rules might actually change how projects operate?
It does suggest communities can force adjustments. That facility moved away from mining after noise problems, which is the kind of outcome the new bills aim to make more predictable.
What about farmland and towns? These centers take up land and could change the character of rural areas.

That's a big part of the conversation. Projects need large parcels, which often means converting farmland, and then there's traffic, noise, and tax revenue questions that hit local budgets differently.
Do the bills address tax breaks or abatements at all?
Not directly from what we've seen, but the larger conversation around them includes how much revenue actually stays local versus what gets negotiated away.
What's the timeline? When could these bills move?
They're just introduced, so committee hearings and debates are next. Nothing is final yet, but the sponsors are framing it as urgent given the pace of proposals.
And on the local side, how many townships have already paused projects while waiting for clearer rules?
We've seen several this week alone, like Lyon Township delaying a vote and Warren considering a moratorium. It's becoming a pattern as residents organize.
Does this state push make those local pauses more or less likely to stick?
It could give them cover. If Lansing sets baselines on power and water, towns might feel more confident setting their own zoning on top of that.
What happens next if the bills pass — do we expect more projects to adjust or fewer to come forward?
Probably both. Developers might redesign for lower impact, but some could look elsewhere if Michigan's rules get stricter than neighboring states.
That brings us to the bigger picture. How does this fit into the national data center boom?
Michigan is one of many states seeing this surge because of AI demand. The bills are an attempt to manage it locally instead of reacting after the fact.
Any sense of how residents are responding so far?
Mixed but vocal. Some welcome the jobs and tax base, others worry about the hidden costs to infrastructure and quality of life.
The recall petitions in Kent County — how unusual is that level of pushback?
It's notable. Usually these debates stay at planning meetings, but recalls show it's reaching a point where voters want to change who makes the decisions.
And the noise fights mentioned — is that the main complaint driving the bills?
It's one of the loudest, along with power and water. The Dowagiac case and the federal lawsuit over nonstop hum show how persistent those issues can be.
So the bills are trying to tackle multiple fronts at once.
That's the idea. Regulating use could indirectly limit the worst impacts without banning projects outright.
What should listeners watch for in the coming weeks?
Committee action on the bills, plus how towns like Lowell and Green Oak handle their own votes tonight and in the days ahead.
Thanks for laying that out. It really shows why this isn't just about one project.
Exactly. Now let's move to some quick hits from across the state today.
Lowell Township residents are voting tonight on a data center project in Kent County. Neighbors want details on noise, traffic, and how much tax revenue actually stays local.
That vote could open the door or set strict limits. It's the first of several decisions this week.
Lyon Township in Oakland County postponed its vote, giving residents more time to review power demand and noise concerns.
The delay means uncertainty lingers, but it also lets locals organize before any final call.
Warren city council is discussing a temporary moratorium on new data centers because of grid strain worries in Macomb County.
That would pause heavy power users while the city reviews its rules.
A local pond near the Oracle and OpenAI campus recovered after dropping during excavation. It shows how fast large digs can affect nearby wells and wetlands.
Residents downstream had been concerned, and the quick rebound is a data point for future projects.

Saline Township's data center campus just closed financing at a $16 billion valuation. That's higher than earlier estimates, which means bigger potential tax revenue but also heavier infrastructure demands.
Neighbors are watching how that affects talks over abatements and road upgrades.
Dowagiac is holding a public forum tonight on data center plans. Concerns center on noise, water, and the electric grid.
It's the first formal chance for residents to ask questions before proposals advance.
Green Oak Township approved new zoning rules and an overlay district for high-resource data centers. Livingston County now has clearer limits on where they can go.
That gives the board more control over electricity and water use going forward.
Yesterday a Michigan town took a data center operator to federal court over nonstop noise. Residents say complaints went nowhere locally and want court relief.
A win there could influence how other communities handle similar hum issues.
And in Mason County, Pere Marquette Township trustees are set to consider a one-year moratorium on new data centers, citing energy, water, and noise impacts.
They're also looking at a $1.36 million park entrance contract at the same meeting.
Now for what's on deck tonight and tomorrow.
Lenox Township Board meets tonight for the first reading of an I-94 Development District ordinance and data center zoning changes amid resident opposition.
Pere Marquette Charter Township is also meeting tonight on that one-year moratorium and the park project.
Lansing City Council has a public hearing on a temporary moratorium for new data center proposals.
Solon Township Board will consider extending its data center moratorium and related ordinances.
And Lansing City Council's Development & Planning committee is holding a public hearing on a 182-day data center moratorium.
Plenty for residents to weigh in on if they're following these issues locally.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.
